stories.headline: Xcel Energy seeks $6.15 monthly rate hike for average Colorado household amid surging disconnections
stories.summary:
Xcel Energy has asked the Colorado Public Utilities Commission to approve a record $225 million revenue increase that would add $6.15 per month to the average residential electric bill, pushing the typical monthly charge from $104.68 to nearly $111. The request comes as Xcel's electric rates have already risen 22.2% since September 2023 — more than three times the rate of inflation — and the average bill has climbed from $90.90 in 2022.

Consumer advocates and PUC staff are raising alarms about affordability, citing a 705% surge in residential disconnections for nonpayment between 2023 and 2025, from 9,731 to 78,337. PUC staff noted that smart meters, for which customers would pay $72 million under the proposed rate case, make remote disconnections easier and may be contributing to the increase.

Xcel Energy CEO Robert Kenney told a legislative committee this spring that 'there is no affordability crisis,' pointing to residential customers paying an average of 1% of household income on electricity. However, the company's own estimates suggest as many as 385,000 customers may be spending 2.5% or more of their income on electricity, and low-income households are spending 10% to 12% on energy costs.

As part of a proposed settlement, Xcel would expand aid to income-qualified families and seniors and reduce shutoffs, but the PUC staff, Energy Outreach Colorado and the Colorado Office of Utility Consumer Advocate say the company is not providing enough money to meet the scale of the need.

stories.headline: Two inmates killed, third injured at Bent County prison in what Colorado DOC now confirms are homicides
stories.summary:
The Colorado Department of Corrections confirmed Monday that two prisoners — Charles Gates, 27, and Michael Fisher, 59 — were killed inside the Bent County Correctional Facility on June 6, and that a third inmate was hospitalized with injuries. A DOC spokeswoman said two suspects have been identified and moved to a more secure facility, but no charges have been filed and the suspects have not been publicly named.

The announcement came exactly one month after the killings at the private, CoreCivic-operated prison in southeastern Colorado. Officials had previously declined to describe the deaths as homicides, and have still refused to disclose how the men were killed, where in the facility the incident occurred, or when the bodies were discovered.

Fisher was serving life without parole for a 1996 felony murder conviction in Adams County, though his attorney had filed a motion shortly after his death noting Fisher was working toward potential release under a 2021 law reclassifying felony murder. Court and prison records show Gates, 27, was serving a different sentence, though details were not specified in the release.

Bent County Sheriff Jake Six said his agency was called to the prison after an 11 p.m. missed inmate count, with deputies securing the perimeter while staff searched inside. The bodies were found after several hours. DOC said additional details will be provided only upon any indictments.

stories.headline: Update: Aspen Acres fire reaches 91,523 acres at 12% containment; at least 180 structures destroyed
stories.summary:
The Aspen Acres fire, burning in the Wet Mountains between Lake San Isabel and Beulah in Custer and Pueblo counties, has grown to 91,523 acres with containment rising to 12% as of Monday morning, according to the Colorado Sun wildfire tracker. The fire, which started June 29 and was determined to be human-caused, is now among the largest in Colorado history and has prompted mandatory evacuations for roughly 3,800 homes affecting an estimated 11,000 people.

At least 180 structures have been confirmed destroyed, a number officials expect to rise as firefighters gain safer access to affected neighborhoods. Mandatory evacuations remain in effect for communities near the Pueblo-Custer county border, including Beulah, Colorado City and Wetmore, with parts of Fremont County added to evacuation orders on July 5.

More than 1,100 personnel were assigned to the fire as of July 5, with a complex incident management team from Alaska having assumed command. Two other major fires are also burning in western Colorado: the Gold Mountain fire near Ouray at 27,698 acres and 3% containment, and the Ferris fire in San Juan National Forest at 47,780 acres and 21% containment.

stories.headline: Wildfire smoke and high ozone push Denver air quality to unhealthy levels; CSU researcher offers guidance
stories.summary:
Wildfire smoke from several large Colorado fires, combined with the Front Range's chronic ozone problem, drove Denver's Air Quality Index to 129 — classified as unhealthy for sensitive groups — on Monday, according to AirNow.gov. The metro Denver area and north Front Range are already designated a severe nonattainment area for federal ozone standards, and wildfire smoke adds chemical precursors that can amplify ozone formation.

Colorado State University public health researcher Sheryl Magzamen, who studies cardiopulmonary effects of smoke on Front Range residents, is leading a NASA-funded study with the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs examining long-term and chronic smoke exposure risks. Magzamen and colleagues describe wildfire smoke as a 'silent epidemic,' noting that particles from distant fires can travel far and penetrate indoors.

Magzamen recommends that residents monitor air quality through AirNow.gov or CDPHE resources and avoid intense outdoor activity on high AQI days. She also advises changing air filters every six months and notes that swamp coolers do not filter air and can actually draw smoke inside, a relevant concern during Colorado summers.

stories.headline: More than two dozen new Colorado laws take effect in July, covering gun control, asset forfeiture and conversion therapy
stories.summary:
More than two dozen Colorado laws took effect July 1, addressing a wide range of topics from firearms regulation to civil asset forfeiture reform and conversion therapy liability. Among the gun-related measures, House Bill 1144 makes it illegal to manufacture 3D-printed guns or firearm components such as large-capacity magazines, with a first violation classified as a misdemeanor and subsequent violations as felonies. A separately enacted 2025 law now requires ammunition shops to keep rounds locked up and generally bans ammo sales to people under 21.

House Bill 1250, backed by a bipartisan coalition, narrows Colorado's civil asset forfeiture rules, limiting law enforcement's ability to keep seized property when no conviction results and establishing a right to legal counsel — with a fund to cover costs for lower-income individuals — for people contesting seizures. The measure was seen as a rare area of agreement between progressive Democrats and conservative Republicans.

House Bill 1322 creates a civil cause of action for people harmed by conversion therapy, allowing them to sue providers who attempted to change their sexual orientation or gender identity. The bill came in response to a U.S. Supreme Court ruling that a prior state ban on the practice violated the First Amendment.

Other new laws include Senate Bill 168, which creates felony-level penalties for wildlife trafficking and adds federally endangered species to existing wildlife protection statutes, as well as measures reforming Medicaid transportation oversight, mail-in ballot timelines for the 2026 general election and a state homeownership assistance program.

stories.headline: Nederland has spent $830,000 in fees as $120 million Eldora ski area purchase remains unclosed
stories.summary:
The small mountain town of Nederland has accumulated more than $830,000 in consulting and legal bills while working to finalize its $120 million purchase of Eldora Mountain Resort, a deal that was supposed to close by April 30 but remains unresolved as of early July. Public records obtained by BusinessDen show the town has retained multiple law firms, some billing as much as $800 an hour, along with resort planning firm Sno-Engineering Inc./SE Group.

Nederland, a community of roughly 1,500 residents, announced a letter of intent to buy the Powdr Corp.-owned ski area in July 2025 and won board approval for the $120 million purchase price in January 2026 — a cost equivalent to about $80,000 per resident. Town Manager Jonathan Cain said the closing will happen 'soon' but cited the complexity of the transaction for the delay, noting the bond financing process remains ongoing.

The town plans to finance the acquisition through revenue bonds to be repaid from Eldora's operating revenue, but Nederland has declined to release the bonds' official statement, denying a public records request from BusinessDen. Powdr Corp. is itself listed as a creditor for $174,000 in early payments related to the acquisition process.

The delay followed a difficult ski season at Eldora and raised questions about the town's financial exposure, particularly because some contracted firms will require payment regardless of whether the deal ultimately closes.

stories.headline: Denver metro median home price climbs to $616,000 as market slows in summer doldrums
stories.summary:
The Denver metro housing market showed a typical seasonal slowdown in June, with the number of homes sold falling 5.56% and pending listings rising 4%, according to the Denver Metro Association of Realtors' latest Market Trends report. Despite slower sales activity, the overall median price rose to $616,000 — about 1% above June 2025 levels — continuing a gradual price recovery following the post-pandemic correction.

Single-family detached home prices climbed 1.5% during June to a median of $675,000, the fifth consecutive monthly increase for that segment across the 11-county metro area. The DMAR report noted that showings are taking longer to generate and agents are following up more frequently with buyers for feedback, signs of a more deliberate market pace.

Upper-end homes are showing particular strength, with closings in the $1.5 million to $1.99 million range up more than 9% over May and closings for homes priced $2 million and above up more than 2%. Condo and attached home prices, by contrast, were down fractionally over the month and 2% over the year.

Brokers say sellers are using rate buydown concessions to maintain strong list prices while helping buyers manage monthly payments in a higher-rate environment. The DMAR report advised sellers that the first 14 days on market remain critical, with homes that do not attract buyers quickly more likely to sit longer and sell at a lower net price.

stories.headline: Colorado State Patrol seeks suspect who beat tow truck driver on I-70 near Limon
stories.summary:
The Colorado State Patrol is asking for the public's help identifying a man who allegedly attacked a tow truck operator on eastbound Interstate 70 just west of Limon on Sunday evening, sending the victim to the hospital with serious injuries. The incident began around 7:33 p.m. when the 51-year-old tow truck driver, who had stopped on the shoulder to assist a disabled vehicle, waved at a passing motorist to move over.

The passing driver stopped his vehicle in front of the tow truck, got out and assaulted the operator before fleeing eastbound on I-70. The suspect was last seen driving a 2007 Dodge Dakota with Colorado license plate AEMF22, pulling a two-wheel car trailer and hauling five tires in the truck bed.

The suspect is described as approximately 6 feet tall and in his mid-50s, with a receding hairline and long hair in back, and was last seen wearing jean overalls and a white T-shirt referencing a Utah national park. CSP is asking anyone with information to call its Pueblo dispatch center at 719-544-2424, referencing case number 3D260690.

stories.headline: Denver police seek silver SUV driver who struck bicyclist in downtown hit-and-run
stories.summary:
Denver Police are searching for the driver of a large silver SUV who struck a bicyclist at 15th and Wynkoop streets in downtown Denver and fled the scene Thursday night. The crash occurred at approximately 9:45 p.m., and police say the bicyclist suffered serious bodily injury.

The vehicle is described as possibly a 2004-2010 Nissan Armada or Infiniti QX56, and may have damage on the front passenger side. Police ask anyone with information to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: Aurora minibike rider dies a week after head-on collision with SUV on East Colfax
stories.summary:
A 23-year-old man has died from injuries sustained when his minibike collided head-on with a Subaru SUV in northeast Aurora on June 29, Aurora police announced Monday. The crash occurred near East Colfax Avenue and North Powhaton Road around 6:45 p.m., when investigators say the minibike rider was traveling west and swerved into oncoming eastbound lanes.

The man was not wearing a helmet at the time of the crash. He was taken to a hospital by paramedics but died one week later; his identity is pending release by the Arapahoe County Coroner's Office.

The Subaru driver remained at the scene and cooperated with investigators. No other injuries were reported, and police did not indicate whether the driver would face charges. The case remains under investigation.

stories.headline: Taco Bell Cantina opens at Denver International Airport, marking state's first alcohol-serving location
stories.summary:
Denver International Airport debuted the state's first Taco Bell Cantina last week near gate A47 in Concourse A, offering alcoholic beverages including beer and boozy Twisted Freezes alongside specialty menu items such as Chicken Fajita Nacho Fries and slow-roasted marinated Cantina chicken. Airport CEO Phil Washington described the opening as both a welcome return of the Taco Bell brand and an economic win in terms of jobs and passenger dining options.

The location is also notable as the first Taco Bell Cantina to open inside any airport in the country. DIA has been expanding its concession offerings as part of a broader strategy to serve a projected 100 million annual passengers.

In other local business news, the Los Dos Potrillos Mexican restaurant in Littleton is set to reopen Wednesday at 10065 W. San Juan Way after several months of repairs following a March 3 kitchen fire. The Ramirez Hospitality Group used the closure as an opportunity to redesign the space, adding a 'wall of traditions' honoring the family's history and recipes. Additionally, Every Little Thing — the health-focused eatery formerly known as Green Collective Eatery — opened its third Denver-area location in Cherry Creek at 2816 E. 3rd Ave.

stories.headline: RTD delays enhanced Broncos game-day bus service to 2027 season
stories.summary:
The Regional Transportation District announced it will not launch its planned enhanced transit service for Denver Broncos home games during the 2026 season, pushing the program back to 2027 after determining further planning adjustments are needed. The RTD board had approved a service plan just last month that would have added more than 10,000 seats on transit vehicles for game days beginning in August at Empower Field at Mile High.

RTD has not operated dedicated direct bus service from Metro Park-N-Ride lots since suspending the service in 2020 during the COVID-19 pandemic. Regular rail service on the E and W lines to the Empower Field at Mile High station has remained the primary transit option in the years since.

The proposed service would rely on designated bus parking lanes and coordinated traffic management plans involving the Broncos organization, the City and County of Denver, CDOT, and Denver Police. Broncos President Damani Leech said the team looks forward to collaborating on transit options for the 2027 season and beyond. RTD General Manager Debra A. Johnson said the delay allows regional partners to build a stronger, more coordinated transit experience for fans.

joke.joke: Being a parent means hiding in the bathroom just to eat a candy bar in peace.

topStory.headline: Iran's Revolutionary Guard Fires Missiles at Commercial Ships Near Strait of Hormuz
topStory.summary:
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps fired at least two missiles at commercial vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz, striking both ships and causing significant damage, according to a U.S. official cited by Axios. No casualties have been reported in connection with the attacks. The incidents represent a serious escalation in an already volatile maritime corridor.

The missile strikes come despite a Memorandum of Understanding signed June 17 between the United States and Iran, which committed both governments to ending hostilities within 60 days. Under that agreement, Iran pledged to restore merchant shipping through the Persian Gulf and Sea of Oman to pre-war volumes within 30 days, while the U.S. agreed to lift its naval blockade against Iran within the same timeframe. The attacks appear to directly contradict Iran's obligations under the MoU.

The broader conflict between the two nations escalated sharply following joint U.S.-Israeli strikes on multiple Iranian sites on February 28. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20 percent of all globally traded oil passes, triggered significant turmoil in international markets and prompted urgent diplomatic efforts to restore stability to the critical waterway.

The fresh missile strikes threaten to derail ongoing negotiations aimed at securing a longer-term peace agreement between Washington and Tehran. Those talks were also intended to address Iran's nuclear program, with Iran having agreed to relinquish enriched uranium reserves capable of being used in weapons development. It remains unclear who ordered the attacks or whether they represent official Iranian government policy, and neither Tehran nor Washington had publicly commented on the incidents at the time of reporting.
